title: Walking an Array, Again
lesson:
And now the punchline. You wrote this exact program on MIPS at the Nintendo 64 level, and again on the LC-3 with LDR. Here it is one more time, in ARM, the architecture in your pocket right now. A pointer, a counter, a running sum. Load through the pointer, add to the sum, advance the pointer by one word (4 bytes), decrement the counter, branch back while it's not zero.

This isn't a coincidence. It's why universities teach load-store architectures: once you can read a for-loop in one of them, you can read it in all of them. The compiler that turned the C in your iOS app into ARM machine code generated a loop almost exactly like this one.
prompt: Sum the five-word array (10, 20, 3, 7, 5 → 45) using a pointer (x1), counter (x2), and running sum (x3). Print the result.
starterCode:
.data
arr:    .word 10, 20, 3, 7, 5
.text
        mov x1, #0x1000      // pointer to first element
        mov x2, #5           // count
        mov x3, #0           // sum
loop:
        ldr x4, [x1]         // load current element
        add x3, x3, x4       // sum += element
        add x1, x1, #4       // advance pointer by 4 bytes (one word)
        sub x2, x2, #1
        cmp x2, #0
        b.ne loop
        mov x0, x3           // copy sum to x0 for printing
        mov x16, #1
        svc #0
        mov x16, #93
        svc #0
hints: mov x1, #0x1000 points x1 at the first array element.
ldr x4, [x1] loads the element x1 points at. add x1, x1, #4 advances to the next.
Use x2 as a count, sub it by 1 each pass, cmp with 0, b.ne back. After the loop, print the sum (45).
successText: The terminal must print 45 (the sum of the array).
reference:
.data
arr: .word 10, 20, 3, 7, 5
.text
mov x1, #0x1000
mov x2, #5
mov x3, #0
loop:
ldr x4, [x1]
add x3, x3, x4
add x1, x1, #4
sub x2, x2, #1
cmp x2, #0
b.ne loop
mov x0, x3
mov x16, #1
svc #0
mov x16, #93
svc #0
